{
  "name": "infinite descent",
  "seminal_paper": {
    "arxiv_id": null,
    "doi": null,
    "citation": "P. de Fermat, Observations sur Diophante, posthumously published in Diophantus, Arithmetica, Toulouse edition.",
    "year": 1670
  },
  "home_domain": "Diophantine equations",
  "what_it_does": "Infinite descent rules out nontrivial integral solutions by showing that any such solution would force the existence of a strictly smaller one of the same kind. It is most effective when the forbidden configuration has a rigid algebraic parametrization and a natural positive integer height.",
  "input_requirements": [
    "an integral Diophantine encoding of the forbidden equally spaced configuration",
    "a well-founded positive height or size attached to every nontrivial solution",
    "a descent map that sends every nontrivial solution to a strictly smaller solution of the same type",
    "integrality or divisibility constraints proving the descended object remains in the same solution class"
  ],
  "output_signature": "nonexistence of nontrivial integer solutions, or finiteness after reduction to finitely many minimal cases",
  "input_match": [
    {
      "requirement": "an integral Diophantine encoding of the forbidden equally spaced configuration",
      "match": "PARTIAL",
      "evidence": "A5 gives an analogy with arithmetic progressions in a power-divisibility-defined sparse set, but no explicit Diophantine encoding is present."
    },
    {
      "requirement": "a well-founded positive height or size attached to every nontrivial solution",
      "match": "PARTIAL",
      "evidence": "A5 suggests ordered consecutive terms nth Powerful k, nth Powerful (k + 1), nth Powerful (k + 2), but no descent height is identified."
    },
    {
      "requirement": "a descent map that sends every nontrivial solution to a strictly smaller solution of the same type",
      "match": "NO",
      "evidence": "no match"
    },
    {
      "requirement": "integrality or divisibility constraints proving the descended object remains in the same solution class",
      "match": "PARTIAL",
      "evidence": "A5 includes power-divisibility structure for powerful numbers, but no preservation mechanism for consecutive powerful triples is available."
    }
  ],
  "known_obstructions": [
    "The target problem concerns three terms rather than four squares, so the classical rigidity of four squares in arithmetic progression is absent.",
    "Powerful numbers form a larger and less rigid class than squares.",
    "The consecutiveness condition is global in the ordered sequence of powerful numbers, not just an algebraic condition on three integers.",
    "No descent map preserving both powerfulness and consecutiveness is supplied by the analog."
  ],
  "fit_score": 0.375,
  "source_analog_id": "A5"
}
